coredata.dc:title: Efficient photocatalytic-fenton oxidation performance of Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods: Structure-activity relationship of photocatalytic degradation of water pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Pengcheng
Wang, Gang
Wen, Jiantong
Li, Xiang
Suo, Yanli
Zhan, Haijuan
Bi, Shuxian
Liu, Wanyi
coredata.dc:description: In this article, the two-dimensional N-doped carbon quantum dot (NCDs) loaded g-C3N4 materials were prepared firstly by microwave and hydrothermal methods (2D g-C3N4/NCDs). The results show that the doping of NCDs improves the charge distribution in the conventional g-C3N4 material and provides more active sites. The catalytic activity of 2D g-C3N4/NCDs material with 2% doping was 3.4 times higher than that of g-C3N4 material. Next, 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs materials were obtained by using Fe3+ ions to induce self-assembly of 2D g-C3N4/NCDs materials under the action of ultrasound. The transformation of the material from 2D to 3D enhances its electron transport capability and visible response range and reduces the band gap value. Meanwhile, the Fenton oxidation effect of Fe ions combined with the photocatalytic effect of the material produced a special photo-Fenton oxidation, which enhanced the oxidation ability of the composite. Thus, 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs materials can degrade a variety of water pollutants (Such as Methyl orange, Rhoda mine B, alizarin red, acidic fuchsine, tetracycline hydrochloride and aureomycin hydrochloride) under light without the addition of additional H2O2, exhibiting excellent catalytic activity (18.75 times of conventional g-C3N4). In addition, the 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs can activate water efficiently to produce active species •O2 − and •OH in situ under visible light, which improved the photocatalytic activity of Fenton-like photocatalyst 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs. The research in this paper improves the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 materials in two dimensions, microscopic electronic structure and macroscopic stereo structure, which will provide new ideas for the application of AOPs in clean production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods
Iron coordination induction
Photo-Fenton reaction
Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs)
Photocatalytic degradation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext primabst ref specialabst

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Major Innovation Projects for Building First-Class Universities in China’s Western Region (No. ZKZD2017003 ), the National First-Rate Discipline Construction Project of Ningxia (No. NXYLXK2017A04 ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22162021 and No. 21862013 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The SEM images: (a) g-C3N4, (b) g-C3N4/NCDs, (c) 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs; TEM images: (d) g-C3N4, (e) g-C3N4/NCDs, (f) 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs; (g-k) The EDS of images of 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs.
Typical nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms for 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs with an inset of the pore size distribution curves.
(a) Powder XRD patterns of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/NCDs and 3D Fe3+/NCDs/g-C3N4; (b) FT-IR spectra of samples g-C3N4, g-C3N4/NCDs and 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs.
The spectra XPS of 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs: (a) Survey scan spectrum; (b) C 1 s; (c) N 1 s; (d) O 1 s; (e) Fe 2p.
(a-b) UV/vis absorption spectra and band gap of samples; (c-d) VB XPS spectra of g-C3N4 and Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs; (e) PL spectra; (f) EIS of the prepared photocatalysts.
(a) (b) Photocatalytic degradation of MO (40 mg of photocatalyst, the MO solution concentration was 20 mg/L); (c) kinetic constant; (d) reusability of 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs.

(a), (b), (c), (d) and (e) curves of photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B (RhB), alizarin red (ARS), acid magenta (AF), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and tunicamycin hydrochloride (CTC) by samples Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs, respectively; (f) rate constants of photocatalytic degradation of various aqueous pollutants by pure phase g-C3N4 and 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs.

(a) Trapping experiment of reactive species; (b) EPR spectra of DMPO-•O2 −; (c) EPR spectra of DMPO-•HO; (d) Schematic diagram of the possible reaction system of the photo-Fenton system on 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs.
Schematic diagram of the possible reaction system of the photo-Fenton system.

head.ce:title: Efficient photocatalytic-fenton oxidation performance of Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods: Structure-activity relationship of photocatalytic degradation of water pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ningxia University, Yinchuan 750021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ningxia University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ningxia University, Yinchuan 750021, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, NingxiaUniversity, Yinchuan 750021, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, NingxiaUniversity

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors (Pengcheng Hao and Gang Wang) contributed equally in this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, the two-dimensional N-doped carbon quantum dot (NCDs) loaded g-C3N4 materials were prepared firstly by microwave and hydrothermal methods (2D g-C3N4/NCDs). The results show that the doping of NCDs improves the charge distribution in the conventional g-C3N4 material and provides more active sites. The catalytic activity of 2D g-C3N4/NCDs material with 2% doping was 3.4 times higher than that of g-C3N4 material. Next, 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs materials were obtained by using Fe3+ ions to induce self-assembly of 2D g-C3N4/NCDs materials under the action of ultrasound. The transformation of the material from 2D to 3D enhances its electron transport capability and visible response range and reduces the band gap value. Meanwhile, the Fenton oxidation effect of Fe ions combined with the photocatalytic effect of the material produced a special photo-Fenton oxidation, which enhanced the oxidation ability of the composite. Thus, 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs materials can degrade a variety of water pollutants (Such as Methyl orange, Rhoda mine B, alizarin red, acidic fuchsine, tetracycline hydrochloride and aureomycin hydrochloride) under light without the addition of additional H2O2, exhibiting excellent catalytic activity (18.75 times of conventional g-C3N4). In addition, the 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs can activate water efficiently to produce active species •O2 − and •OH in situ under visible light, which improved the photocatalytic activity of Fenton-like photocatalyst 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs. The research in this paper improves the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 materials in two dimensions, microscopic electronic structure and macroscopic stereo structure, which will provide new ideas for the application of AOPs in clean production.

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) are effective tools for the degradation of water pollutant such as dyes and antibiotics [1]. Among them, as one of the advanced oxidation processes, the heterogeneous Fenton process can overcome the shortcomings of homogeneous catalysis such as sludge formation and non-recycle of catalysts. It has become one of the most effective methods for pollutant degradation, with the advantage of wider application [1,2]. However, as the heterogeneous Fenton reactions are always restricted by the unsatisfied reduction efficiency of Fe(III), the difficulty of Fe(II) regeneration and the high consumption of H2O2 [2]. Therefore, different types of catalysts have been continuously developed by researchers to improve the above problems, such as carbons/ferrihydrite [2], Hematite/selenium disulfide hybrid catalyst [3], NH2-MIL-101 (Fe) [4], biochar/CaO2 [5], iron-doped biochar [6]. These materials provide very useful inspirations for accelerating the iron redox cycle to construct an efficient Fenton-like system, but the construction of a catalyst for Fenton oxidation with higher performance and a wider range of applications is still a challenging task.

Photocatalysis is also a kind of advanced oxidation processes [7–10]. It is worth noting that the photocatalytic is superior in terms of high efficiency, clean energy and sustainable in mild environment [11–14]. The combination of photocatalytic materials and Fenton oxidizing materials brings new vitality to the degradation of water pollutants [15]. Based on the interfacial charge transfer effect (IFCT), Fang et al. prepared Fe(III)/CQDs/Fe-CN catalyst, which will produce Fenton effect under visible light irradiation [16]. The IFCT effect and the photo-Fenton effect together promote the degradation of MO and phenol, but the small specific surface area and narrow visible light absorption range limit its development. Moreover, Wu et al. [13] recently reported that the α-FeOOH/CQDs/g-C3N4 was constructed by coupling the g-C3N4/CQDs with Fe2+ metal ions. The energy band structure of g-C3N4 and the photoelectron trapping ability were effectively regulated, the result shows it has excellent degradation effect on organic dyes pollutants in wastewater. However, the performance of this material still requires a considerable amount of H2O2 as an oxidant, its essence is the photocatalytic oxidation degradation mechanism of Fenton, which limits its potential application due to cost. The latest research shows that N-doped nanostructured carbon materials are of great interest because of their improved electron transfer and extension of the visible light adsorption region [17–20]. The intervention of N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) may further promote the activity of the photo-Fenton catalyst. Because the introduction of NCDs can add more active coordination sites to the composite, it enhances the oxidation capacity of g-C3N4 by promoting the charge transfer process of the conjugated π-π-structure at the NCDs-C3N4 interface [15,16,21], together with the Fenton oxidation of Fe. In addition, it has been documented that NCDs can significantly increase the light absorption region and reduce the charge transport distance of a photocatalyst by adjusting the charge distribution in the material [14,20,22]. However, as far as we know, the use of Fe3+-mediated N-doped CQDs to modify g-C3N4 materials (Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs) as photo-Fenton catalysts has never been reported.

N-containing carbon-based materials have potential applications in the field of photocatalytic degradation because of their special electronic structure [23,24]. However, most of the materials synthesized by conventional methods are two-dimensional (2D) lamellar materials, the thicker volume of which hinders the generation and conduction of photoelectrons and affects their catalytic activity. Carbon materials with a three-dimensional structure (3D) have been shown to be a high-performance optoelectronic and topological material with great potential for applications in optoelectronics, photovoltaics, quantum topology materials and catalysis [25]. But, the need for additional templating agents or more complex preparation processes for 3D materials greatly limits their further application. Therefore, the study of simple ways to induce the transformation of materials from 2D to 3D is of great value.

Herein, we report a new process for the preparation of Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs ternary composites: N-doped carbon nano-quantum dots (NCDs) were prepared by microwave method using citric acid and urea as raw materials; Two-dimensional NCDs-loaded g-C3N4 materials were obtained by hydrothermal compounding of NCDs with g-C3N4 obtained by melamine pyrolysis (2D g-C3N4/NCDs); Self-assembly of 2D g-C3N4/NCDs materials induced by Fe ions under ultrasonication to form 3D rod composites (3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs). The material showed promising results in the photocatalytic degradation of a variety of aqueous pollutants without the addition of additional H2O2. The results show that the doping of NCDs adjusts the charge distribution in g-C3N4 materials and provides more coordination active sites; the introduction of Fe ions induces the transformation from 2D to 3D materials, and its special photo-Fenton effect enhances the redox ability of the materials. The research in this paper improves the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 materials in two dimensions, microscopic electronic structure and macroscopic stereo structure, which will provide new ideas for the application of AOPs in clean production.

The change of the material morphology and structure will have a great impact on the catalytic performance of the material [26,27]. First, the morphology and structure of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/NCDs and 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs materials were observed by SEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1. From Fig. 1(a), it can be found that the traditional g-C3N4 material has a sheet-like structure with thicker sheet spacing. Fig. 1(b) shows the SEM images of g-C3N4/NCDs material loaded with NCDs by hydrothermal method. Since the composite material was prepared by reacting at 180 °C for 5 h in a hydrothermal kettle, it can be clearly found that its morphology and structure are different from the traditional g-C3N4 material. The lamellae of the g-C3N4/NCDs material are thinner, while the surface of the material has more granular substances and the surface color is darker. This change may occur because under this synthetic condition, NCDs can largely intercalate and exfoliate bulk g-C3N4 structure efficiently (i.e., carbon dots are dispersed into the interlayer of g-C3N4 without destroying the framework). thereby widening the layer distance of g-C3N4 [27–29].

In addition, when iron ions were introduced into the g-C3N4/NCDs material, the morphology and structure of the Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs material changed completely (Fig. 1(c)). In order to better study the effect of different contents of iron ions on the morphology of the materials, the morphology changes of Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs materials with different iron doping contents were observed by SEM (Fig. S1). It can be clearly seen from Fig. S1 that with the increase of Fe3+ content, the rod-like structure of 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs catalyst becomes more obvious. When the iron content was increased to 5%, the rod-like structures in the material exhibited obvious stacking and aggregation. The image of the material with 4% doping is shown in Fig. 1(c), and it can be found that each nanorod is about 40 nm in diameter. This well shows that the addition of iron ions can induce the self-assembly of traditional g-C3N4 materials to form 3D nanorod structures. During the reaction, Fe ions will complex and coordinate with functional groups such as carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups on the g-C3N4/NCDs template (to form Fe-O, Fe-N chemical bonds) to adjust the aggregation state of the g-C3N4 material molecular chain, and finally formed neatly arranged 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods through self-assembly.

From the HRTEM images, it can be found that compared with the g-C3N4 material alone (Fig. 1(d)), many darker dots (Fig. 1(e)) are observed in the image of the g-C3N4/NCDs material (Fig. 1(e)). It is thought to be formed by the shuttle introduction of NCDs [29]. In addition, lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.233 nm were found in the HRTEM image of the 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs material, which were attributed to the 110 crystal plane of the iron ions introduced into the material. Finally, the element composition and distribution on the surface of 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs material were analyzed by EDS. In the energy spectrum of the elements, it can be observed that the material contains C, H, O, N, Fe and other elements (Fig. 1(g)). The strong diffraction peak at 2.0 kev energy is attributed to the characteristic peak of the element Pt. This is due to the poor electrical conductivity of the material and the spraying of Pt elements on the surface of the sample in order to obtain a clearer morphological structure. It can be found from Fig. 1(h-k) that the distribution of various elements in the 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs material is uniform, especially the iron element. This indicates that the iron ions are well dispersed in the material without obvious aggregation. This excellent dispersion state may bring better catalytic activity.

The specific surface areas of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/NCDs, 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs and 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs samples after repeated use for 4 times were 8.981, 47.259, 37.650 and 35.468 m2g−1, respectively ( Table 1). Compared with g-C3N4, the binary material g-C3N4/NCDs has a higher BET surface area, which is due to the increased pore size under the action of the solvent during the hydrothermal process. The increase of BET surface area indicates that the material has abundant pore size and strong physical adsorption capacity [30]. At the same time, it also means that the incident frequency of light can be increased and the transmission path of electrons can be extended. It is worth noting that the BET surface area and micropore volume of the sample 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs are slightly decreased compared with the binary g-C3N4/NCDs, which is because the introduction of Fe species will cover the surface to a certain extent. The catalyst pores were observed, but the average pore size changed little, indicating that the overall structure of the catalyst remained intact. In addition, the photocatalytic performance of the catalyst is not that the larger the BET specific surface area [31], the better, but only the structure that matches the performance of the material itself can be more conducive to photocatalysis. At the same time, the BET surface area and micropore volume of the catalyst decreased after 4 times of use, which can be attributed to the inevitable attachment of some dye molecules and solvent molecules to the catalyst surface and pores during the degradation process [32] ( Fig. 2).

The structural properties of Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs were determined by nitrogen adsorption-desorption experiments. The sample Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs showed a typical type IV curve with H3 type hysteresis loop at high pressure according to the IUPAC classification, which indicated that the sample was formed by the aggregation of mesoporous particles with irregular flat slit structure [33]. The BET surface area of the sample Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs is about 37.650 m2g−1 and the average pore size is 10.191 nm. The pore size distribution of the composites was further estimated using the Barrett-Joy-ner-Halenda (BJH) method (inset), showing that the pore size of the mesoporous particles is mainly around 10–50 nm. The relatively large surface area and abundant mesoporous structure at the nanoscale are beneficial to improve the adsorption of pollutants on the composite surface and accumulate more light energy during the degradation process. Consistent with the SEM and TEM conclusions.

Subsequently, the crystal and chemical composition of the above samples were further examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD). It can be seen from the XRD patterns ( Fig. 3. (a)) that g-C3N4, g-C3N4/NCDs and 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs with different iron contents all have two different peaks at 13.0° and 27.4°, which correspond to the (100) and (002) crystal planes of g-C3N4 respectively [1,11,12]. The diffraction peak of NCDs was not found in the atlas due to the reflection between graphite-like structure layers [28,34], which may be attributed to the fact that trace NCDs are inserted and dispersed in g-C3N4 in nanoscale state. It can be seen from the map that with the increase of iron content, the diffraction peak at the (002) crystal plane of g-C3N4 will gradually weaken. This phenomenon is attributed to the fact that the preferred binding point of ferric doping is at the interface of the (002) crystal plane, which leads to the directional anchoring effect of iron in 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs complex. In other words, the template effect formed by g-C3N4 molecular chain interface and NCDs supramolecular shuttle agent is mainly mediated by the coordination effect between functional groups at the interface and iron ions, and is a self-assembly mechanism occurring in confined space [29,34,35]. In addition, no diffraction peaks for α-FeOOH and α-FeO were found in the XRD pattern of the material (Fig. S2). This is obviously different from the known literature that α-FeOOH/CQDs/g-C3N4 has new diffraction peaks α-FeOOH and α-FeO in the XRD pattern [13], and it is believed that the doping of Fe and CQDs will greatly change the overall structure of g-C3N4. However, our’s results indicating that the doping of trace Fe and trace NCDs did not change the chemical structure of g-C3N4 molecular chain (bulk chemical structure) [13,15], but indeedly, the microscopic morphology has undergone significant transformation, namely from the thin-layer 2D nanoplates of g-C3N4/NCDs to 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods. There are two reasons for the absence of distinct iron peaks in the XRD of materials with different iron contents: First of all, the content is low and not sufficient to cause change. Usually when trace amounts of iron are loaded onto carbon-based materials (C3N4 type), due to the amorphous structure of the carbon material, the XRD tests of the composites show mainly broad peaks of the C3N4 material. Therefore, the characteristic peaks of the iron species are not evident. Secondly, the added iron is uniformly dispersed in the material without obvious aggregation. Because of its small amount of addition and uniform dispersion, there is no obvious characteristic peak of iron in XRD. Secondly, iron will interact with elements such as N and C in the material, thereby affecting the relative peak heights of the 100 and 002 crystal planes in the C3N4 material. This slight change in XRD only confirms that the Fe species has been successfully loaded and interacted with the material. It indicates trace Fe3+ and trace NCDs play a key role in regulating g-C3N4 microscopic morphology, and this is also the key to its excellent photoelectric characteristics.

Furthermore, the above-mentioned structural characteristics of the surface chemical bond composition and element distribution that effectively adjust the microscopic morphology and photoelectric characteristics of g-C3N4 were revealed by the FTIR spectra of pure g-C3N4, g-C3N4/NCDs and 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs doped with different Fe contents are shown in Fig. 3. (b). The spectra of pure phase g-C3N4, g-C3N4/NCDs and 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs were found to show broad peaks within 3000–3500 cm−1, which were attributed to C-OH and N-H stretching vibrations, respectively [36], the absorption peak at 1461 cm−1 was due to the absorption vibration of CN, the 1417 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching and bending vibrations of C-H, and the absorption peaks at 1250 cm−1 and 1328 cm−1 are typical of the stretching vibrations of the g-C3N4 heterocyclic structure. Compared with the pure phase g-C3N4, we can see that absorption peaks at 1461 cm−1, 1417 cm−1, 1328 cm−1 and 1250 cm−1 in the spectrum of g-C3N4/NCDs and Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs have a slight blue shift (Fig. S3). This may be attributed to the strong interfacial supramolecular interaction between NCDs doping and g-C3N4, including the regulation of electronic structure by charge, electrostatic and hydrogen bonding [37,38]. When Fe element is introduced, new absorption peaks appeared at 1102 cm−1 and 622 cm−1, which can be attributed to Fe-N and Fe-O stretching vibrations, respectively [16,39]. This can be attributed to the coordination between Fe element and O, N element in g-C3N4/NCDs composite, which further regulates the surface electronic structure of the material.

At the same time, the surface element distribution and chemical bond composition was further confirmed by XPS full spectrum analysis ( Fig. 4, and Fig. S4), that is, C, N, O and Fe elements all exist in the nanorods materials, which is consistent with IR and EDS analysis results. It is noteworthy that there are two typical wide peaks in the Fe 2 P XPS map at 710 eV and 724 eV (Fig. 4, e), corresponding to Fe 2 P1/2 and Fe 2p2/3 respectively, which prove that the valence of Fe is trivalent [16,40]. And it shuttles on the surface of g-C3N4/NCDs molecular chain, as Fe-O, Fe-N the coordination form of micro-nano cluster shape appears, so it has a wide peak. This further shows that with the help of the nano interface of highly dispersed NCDs, a small amount of Fe elements shuttle to the surface of the molecular chain of g-C3N4/NCDs through the formation of Fe-O and Fe-N coordination bonds [37,38], and is one of the key ways to optimize and improve the structural characteristics as well as photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 semiconductor materials.

In a nutshell, a novel visible-light-driven Fe3+-mediated N-doped carbon dot (NCDs) 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods with special micro morphology and 3D morphological structure was successfully synthesized by loading N-doped CQDS nanoparticles, Fe3+ was added to the sandwich and surface of g-C3N4 by a simple hydrothermal and ultrasonic impregnation method, respectively. The above-mentioned intrinsic characteristics may be the essence of the performance of the 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs that is, the key to high-efficiency photodegradation performance under hydrogen peroxide-free for organic dyes caused by its structure.

The UV-DRS, photoluminescence (PL) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/NCDs and Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs. UV–vis ( Fig. 5, a-b) diffuse reflectance test showed that the intrinsic absorption edge of g-C3N4/NCDs showed obvious red shift compared with pure g-C3N4, and the UV absorption capacity and red shift effect of g-C3N4/NCDs were relatively best when Fe doping amount was 4% of the total mass ratio. Compared with pure g-C3N4 (2.70 eV), the band gap of 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods (2.56 eV) doped with 2% NCDs and 4% Fe decreases by 0.14 eV. The narrow band gap can increase the utilization rate of visible light, thus generating more photoelectron-hole pairs under visible light irradiation. According to the calculation of VB-XPS spectra, the valence band (VB) values are 1.62 eV and 1.92 eV (Fig. 4, c-d), respectively. According to the formula Ec= Ev-Eg, CB of g-C3N4 and 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods are − 1.08 eV and − 0.64 eV, respectively. The more positive the REDOX potential of the valence band is, the more negative the REDOX potential of the conduction band is, and the stronger the REDOX capacity of photogenerated holes (h+) and photogenerated electrons (e-) is. Therefore, the good light absorption capacity and narrow band gap of ternary photocatalyst 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods are determined.

Fig. 4(e) shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra to investigate the photo-induced charge recombination. It can be seen from photoluminescence (PL) spectrum that g-C3N4/NCDs shows a lower PL intensity compared with pure g-C3N4, while 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods material has a lower PL intensity. Moreover, the blue shift phenomenon (attributed to the decrease of band gap energy after modification of NCDs by Fe) appears, indicating that the appropriate amount of Fe doping can effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. However, the PL spectrum of catalyst α-FeOOH/CQDs/g-C3N4 reported in literature [13] does not shift, indicating that the Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods with new structure has better absorption of visible light. In conclusion, these results further indicate that trace Fe and trace NCDs not only significantly regulate the morphology, surface structure and surface intrinsic electronic properties of substrate g-C3N4, but also significantly regulate the 3D surface intrinsic electronic properties of substrate g-C3N4. Moreover, the modified g-C3N4 nanorods (3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs) also have a positive effect on the effective separation of photoelectron-hole pair and the migration of photocarriers, which will significantly enhance the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst.

The transport process of photogenerated carriers of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/NCDs and 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs was further studied by electrochemical impedance (EIS). As shown in Fig. 4(f), the arc radius in the high frequency region of pure g-C3N4 is the largest among the three materials, indicating that the impedance is the largest in the photocatalytic process and the electron-hole pair has the worst charge transfer ability. Compared with pure g-C3N4, the radius of high frequency arc in g-C3N4/NCDs becomes smaller, indicating that the charge transfer rate is obviously improved. It is worth noting that when trace Fe is introduced, the arc radius of the high frequency region of the catalyst 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs is much smaller than that of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/NCDs, which means that the catalyst has a smaller impedance and faster charge transfer in the photocatalytic process. These results are consistent with the above PL results, indicating that Fe and NCDS modified g-C3N4 matrix composite nanorods can significantly promote photocharge transfer and separation, thus effectively enhancing the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysis.

The photocatalytic performance of the obtained samples g-C3N4, g-C3N4/NCDs and 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs was assessed by the ability to degrade MO. For comparison purposes, the dark phase of the material was set to 30 min, after which the light source was switched on for testing. All materials were tested under these conditions. As can be seen from Fig. 5(a, b), without H2O2, (1) the adsorption capacity of photocatalyst increases with the increase of NCDs doping. This can be attributed to the enhanced π-π interaction between the structure of NCDs and pollutants [13,16,41]. (2) Under the action of light, the photocatalytic performance of the sample doped with NCDs is obviously better than that of pure g-C3N4. This is because the doping of NCDs leads to the enhancement of visible light response region and the decrease of the coincidence rate of photogen-electron pair holes [13,15,16]. However, when the doping amount of NCDs is further increased, the photocatalytic degradation rate will decrease due to the “shielding effect” of NCDs [16]. Therefore, the photocatalytic degradation activity of g-C3N4 doped with appropriate amount of NCDs was significantly higher than that of pure g-C3N4, and the degradation rate of MO dye was up to 66.9% within 120 min (the degradation rate of pure g-C3N4 was 22.7%). In addition, in order to fully illustrate the accuracy of the above-mentioned experimental results, ensure the correctness of the experimental analysis. We also performed the degradation experiments of Rh B with different doping amounts of g-C3N4/NCDs materials (as shown in the Fig. S5), and the photocatalytic degradation test results were consistent with the above performance. For comparison purposes, the dark phase of the material was set to 30 min, after which the light source was switched on for testing. All materials were tested under these conditions. (3) As can be seen from Fig. 5 (b), the rate of photocatalytic degradation of MO by further doping g-C3N4, Fe doped samples show excellent photocatalytic performance. When the Fe content was increased from 1% to 4%, it was clear that the photocatalytic performance of the samples increased with the Fe content. This may be attributed to two aspects: firstly, after adding Fe ions to the material, the iron ions induce the self-assembly of traditional 2D g-C3N4 materials to form 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods, and this structural change is certain Extensive tuning of the band structure of the composites [15,16]. Secondly, it is worth mentioning that Fe element has a special Fenton oxidation effect, and traditional iron-based materials need to have this characteristic in the presence of H2O2. However, due to the unique light-responsive properties of 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods, H2O2 can be generated in situ by activating water under illumination, resulting in Fenton oxidation (Fig. S5). Therefore, the introduction of iron ions improves the catalytic performance of the material from two aspects of electronic structure and Fenton oxidation, so the photocatalytic degradation performance of 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods for MO is 3.4 times that of traditional g-C3N4. However, when Fe doping continues to increase to 5%, the photocatalytic degradation performance of the sample is almost unchanged, which can be attributed to the limited active sites on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheet. Excessive Fe doping will cover the active sites on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheet, weakening the light collection ability of 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods.

In conclusion, based on the above results, we can conclude that the photocatalytic performance of the g-C3N4 3D morphology sample is much higher than that of g-C3N4’s 2D and 1D morphology sample. It's important to emphasize that, this is just a small amount of Fe3+ and NCDs play a key role in regulating g-C3N4 microscopic morphology. It also proves that the interface of g-C3N4 matrix composites which can be controlled by nano-dimension often determines the separation and migration efficiency of photoelectrons and holes, and is an effective way to improve the photocatalytic performance [42]. In addition, the photocatalytic degradation kinetics of Methyl orange (MO) by 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods conform to first-order linear correlation, as shown in the Fig. 5 (c). The calculated results show that the rate constant of methyl orange degradation by unit catalyst g-C3N4 is 0.0020 min−1, and the rate constant of MO degradation by binary catalyst g-C3N4 /NCDs is 0.0068 min−1, which is 3.4 times that of g-C3N4. The degradation rate constant of MO by 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods catalyst is 0.0375 min−1, and the degradation rate constant is 18.75 times that of g-C3N4. The 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs photocatalyst was tested for its stability and reusability after four cycles of degradation experiments (Fig. 5, d)). After four consecutive cycles, the degradation activity of 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs still maintained a high photocatalytic performance, indicating its excellent stability.

To evaluate the general applicability of the photocatalytic degradation performance of the samples 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs, other types of dyes Rhoda mine B (RhB), Alizarin Red (AZS) and Acid Magenta (AF), antibiotic-like aqueous contaminants tetracycline hydrochloride (TCHC) and tunicamycin hydrochloride (AMHC) were selected for photocatalytic degradation tests ( Fig. 6, a-e). The 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs nanorods catalyst resulted in a 13.13 (RHB), 5.63 (AZR), 12.91 (AF), 2.65 (TCHC) and 2.43 (AMHC) fold higher reaction rate than that of pristine g-C3N4 (Fig. 6, f)). It can be concluded that the samples 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs have general applicability for the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants.

To investigate the mechanism of ternary photocatalytic degradation of pollutants, active species capture experiments and electron spin resonance technique (EPR) tests were performed. IPA, EDTA-2Na and BQ were used as hydroxyl radical (·OH), hole (h+) and superoxide radical (·O2 -) capture agents, respectively. The results are shown in the Fig. 7 (a), showing the ability of different free radical trapping agents to inhibit MO degradation in the order of BQ﹥IPA﹥EDTA-2Na. It can be seen that the addition of EDTA-2Na has almost no inhibitory effect in the degradation process, indicating that cavities (h+) are not the main active species in the degradation process. While the degradation rate became significantly lower in the reaction with the addition of BQ and IPA, which implies that ·O2 - and ·OH play a decisive role in the degradation process. Electron spin resonance (EPR) technique was further used to demonstrate that ·O2 - and ·OH are the main contributors in the degradation process. As shown in the Fig. 7 (b, c), the fluctuation of the EPR signal was almost negligible in the dark, however, under visible illumination, both DMPO-·OH and DMPO-·O2 - showed a strong four-line characteristic EPR signal, which indicated that ·O2 - and ·OH were generated during photocatalytic degradation, in agreement with the results of the radical trapping experiments. Moreover, the EPR signal was present at both 2 min and 4 min under visible light irradiation and showed a stronger EPR signal at 4 min, which implies that the production of ·O2 - and ·OH is continuous during the degradation process [43]. Therefore, combining the results of radical trapping experiments and EPR tests, it can be concluded that ·O2 - and ·OH are the main active species in the photocatalytic degradation process. In addition, Ar2 was introduced into the MO solution and confined space during the degradation process to remove O2 from the air and water to detect the source of ·O2 -. The results showed (Fig. S6) that the degradation rate did not decrease after the exclusion of O2 in air and water, indicating that the ·O2 - in the solution might be generated by the activated water (
Fig. 8,9).

Based on the above experimental results and analysis, the reaction mechanism of 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs materials in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants can be reasonably proposed. In the photocatalytic system, the photocatalyst 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs was excited under visible light to generate photogenerated electron-hole pairs. NCDs can act as electron acceptors and can inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. A part of the photogenerated electrons can be quickly transferred to Fe3+ to form Fe2+ in the catalyst. The resulting Fe2+ reacts with H2O2 generated from in situ activated H2O to generate hydroxyl radical·OH [44]. Another part of the electrons can be transferred to NCDs through π-π conjugation, and then the electrons can directly decompose H2O2 to form·OH [45]. After a series of reactions, MO finally generates CO2 and H2O. In addition, the H2O2 generated in the system can also react with holes to generate O2, thereby providing raw materials for the generation of·O2 -. The traditional homogeneous or heterogeneous Fenton system usually generates Fe3+ and hydroxyl radical·OH through the oxidation of Fe2+ with in situ generated H2O2. In contrast, the photocatalytic-fenton system can excite electrons to facilitate the conversion of Fe3+ to Fe2+, and this Fe3+/Fe2+ redox cycle can significantly improve the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants by the Fenton-like reaction, and can be achieved to a certain extent. to reduce the consumption of H2O2 [46].

In summary, we report a new process for the preparation of Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs ternary composites: N-doped carbon nano-quantum dots (NCDs) were prepared by microwave method using citric acid and urea as raw materials; Two-dimensional NCDs-loaded g-C3N4 materials were obtained by hydrothermal compounding of NCDs with g-C3N4 obtained by melamine pyrolysis (2D g-C3N4/NCDs); Self-assembly of 2D g-C3N4/NCDs materials induced by Fe ions under ultrasonication to form 3D rod composites (3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs). The experimental results show that the doping of NCDs improves the charge distribution in the conventional g-C3N4 material and provides more active sites. The catalytic activity of 2D g-C3N4/NCDs material with 2% doping was 3.4 times higher than that of g-C3N4 material. The trace Fe3+ and trace NCDs play a key role in regulating g-C3N4 microscopic morphology. Among them, with the help of the nano interface of highly dispersed NCDs, a small amount of Fe elements shuttle to the surface of the molecular chain of 2D g-C3N4/NCDs through the formation of Fe-O and Fe-N coordination bonds. The transformation of the material from 2D to 3D enhances its electron transport capability and visible response range and reduces the band gap value. Meanwhile, the Fenton oxidation effect of Fe ions combined with the photocatalytic effect of the material produced a special photo-Fenton oxidation, which enhanced the oxidation ability of the composite. Thus, 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs materials can degrade a variety of water pollutants (Such as Methyl orange (MO), Rhoda mine B (RHB), alizarin red (AZR), acidic fuchsine (AF), tetracycline hydrochloride (TCHC) and aureomycin hydrochloride (AMHC)) under light without the addition of additional H2O2, exhibiting excellent catalytic activity (18.75 times of conventional g-C3N4). Superoxide radicals (·O2 -) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) generated by 3D Fe3+/NCDs/g-C3N4 by efficient activation of water under visible light are the main active species in the degradation process. The research in this paper improves the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 materials in two dimensions, microscopic electronic structure and macroscopic stereo structure, which will provide new ideas for the application of AOPs in clean production.

Referring to the literature method, 5 g of melamine was placed in a crucible with a lid, after which it was transferred to a muffle furnace and then heated at a rate of 5 °C/min to 550 °C and held for 2 h. After natural cooling, the sample g-C3N4 was obtained.

The method for the preparation of NCDs by reference was improved [24]. Briefly, 3 g of urea and 3 g of citric acid were added to 10 mL of water and then irradiated in a microwave oven on medium-high heat (540 W) for 5 min. The obtained solid was placed in an oven at 60 °C for 60 min to eliminate volatile molecules. The obtained NCDs powder was dissolved in 180 mL of water, then the coalescing particles of the NCDs solution were eliminated by centrifugation for 10 min (8000 r/min) to obtain a brown aqueous solution (which contained 5 mg/mL of NCDs).

The method of reference was improved. The g-C3N4/NCDs were prepared by hydrothermal method [23]. Briefly, 1 g of g-C3N4 and a certain amount of NCDs (the mass of NCDs is 0, 0.5w%, 1w%, 1.5w%, 2w%,3w% of the mass of g-C3N4) were taken and stirred for 10 min and poured into an autoclave at 180 °C, held for 5 h, filtered, dried, and obtained binary composite photocatalysts g-C3N4/NCDs series products (labeled as g-C3N4/NCDs-Xw%) were prepared and evaluated.

On the basis of the above study, the best photocatalytic degradation effect of g-C3N4/NCDs-2% (the mass of NCDs was 2% of the total mass) was taken, and then a certain amount of Fe(NO3)3-9 H2O (iron nitrate nine-water was added to the mass of g-C3N4/NCDs hundred percent (0w%, 1w%,2w%, 3w%, 4w%, 5%). The ternary composites 3D Fe3+/g-C3N4/NCDs were obtained by ultrasonication for 5 min and then placed in an oil bath at 80 °C with stirring to allow the deionized water to evaporate naturally, and then dried in an oven overnight (labeled as g-C3N4/NCDs/Fe-Xw%), standby and for evaluation.

The photocatalytic activity of the ternary composite photocatalytic materials was evaluated by degrading the organic pollutants methyl orange (MO), Rhoda mine B (Rh B), alizarin red (AZR), acid magenta (AF), and the small molecule environmental pollutants tetracycline hydrochloride (TCHC) and aureomycin hydrochloride (AMHC). A 350 W halogen lamp is used as the light source. The temperature was maintained at 22 °C using an ethanol circulation system, and 50 mg of catalyst was first dispersed in 50 mL of 20 mg/L contaminant solution, and then the contaminant solution was stirred in the dark for 40 min to reach adsorption equilibrium; 3 mL of supernatant was aspirated at specified time intervals every 20 min during photocatalysis, and the catalyst was removed by filtering with a 0.22 µm microporous filter tip. The solution was measured by UV-Vis spectrophotometer for absorbance at λ = 463 nm (the maximum absorption wavelength of MO is 463 nm). The photocatalytic degradation procedures of Rhoda mine B (Rh B), alizarin red (AZS), acid magenta (AF), and tetracycline hydrochloride (TCHC) and aureomycin hydrochloride (AMHC) were performed under the same conditions as MO.

Pengcheng Hao: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review and editing. Gang Wang: Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Jiantong Wen: Validation. Xiang Li: Formal analysis. Yanli Suo: Formal analysis. Haijuan Zhan: Project administration, Visualization, Writing – review and editing. Shuxian Bi: Visualization, Supervision. Wanyi Liu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Major Innovation Projects for Building First-Class Universities in China’s Western Region (No. ZKZD2017003), the National First-Rate Discipline Construction Project of Ningxia (No. NXYLXK2017A04) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22162021 and No. 21862013).
